title: Listening - Part 4: Writing Film Subtitles
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Tom Reyes, a film subtitler. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m talking to Tom Reyes, who writes subtitles for films and television programmes. Tom, how did you start doing this? || Tom: I studied languages and expected to work in tourism. Then a friend making a short student film wanted to enter an international festival. [24]I offered to produce English subtitles for the film, and I enjoyed the challenge of making the characters understandable without losing their personality[/24]. I later took a specialist course and began assisting an experienced subtitler. || Presenter: Surely it is mainly a matter of translating dialogue? || Tom: Translation is the foundation, but viewers also have to read while watching the screen. [25]The hardest part is expressing an idea naturally in far fewer words, so the subtitle disappears before it becomes a distraction[/25]. A perfect translation that stays on screen too long is not useful. There is always a balance between detail, timing and allowing viewers to watch the actors. || Presenter: What happens with jokes? || Tom: Jokes are particularly interesting, because a direct translation often makes no sense in another culture. [26]Rather than translating each word, I try to find a line that gives viewers the same reason to laugh at the same moment[/26]. Occasionally a joke cannot be saved, but that is rarer than people think. || Presenter: How do you know whether your work is successful? || Tom: Usually I deliver files alone from my office, so I don’t see an audience. At festivals I sometimes attend a screening. [27]Hearing viewers laugh or become silent at exactly the intended point tells me the subtitles are supporting the film rather than getting in its way[/27]. That response is enormously satisfying. || Presenter: Do automatic translation tools help? || Tom: They can suggest a basic meaning quickly, especially for simple factual speech. But stories are full of relationships, politeness and hidden intentions. [28]A machine may translate the words accurately yet completely miss that a character is being sarcastic or affectionate[/28]. I use technology carefully, never as the final judge. || Presenter: Is this a lonely job? || Tom: There are long hours at a computer, certainly, but I am not isolated from the production. [29]When a character’s intention is unclear, I discuss it with the director or writer rather than making an unsupported guess[/29]. Those conversations often reveal details even the original audience might miss. || Presenter: What is your advice for beginners? || Tom: Many language students immediately begin translating the dialogue list. [30]I recommend watching the entire film first, because you cannot subtitle a scene properly without understanding the story and the relationships[/30]. Good subtitles come from watching as well as writing.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: How did Tom first become involved in subtitling?

options.A: A lecturer found him work at a film festival.
options.B: He helped a friend with a student film.
options.C: He translated tourist information for a cinema.

questions.explanation: Tom made English subtitles for a friend's student film entered in an international festival.

questions.question: What does Tom find most difficult about subtitling?

options.A: Using fewer words while keeping the meaning natural.
options.C: Deciding how long each film scene should last.

questions.explanation: The audience needs brief natural subtitles that do not distract from the film.

questions.question: How does Tom usually deal with humour?

options.A: He removes jokes which depend on culture.
options.B: He explains jokes using additional subtitles.
options.C: He looks for a line creating a similar comic effect.

questions.explanation: Rather than word-for-word translation, he aims for viewers to laugh at the same point.

questions.question: What does Tom enjoy about attending film festivals?

options.A: Meeting other professionals who need subtitles.
options.B: Observing whether the audience reacts at the intended moments.
options.C: Explaining translation choices after a screening.

questions.explanation: Audience laughter or silence at the appropriate moment shows his work supports the film.

questions.question: What limitation of automatic translation does Tom mention?

options.A: It cannot deal with factual dialogue.
options.B: It is too slow for television work.
options.C: It can fail to recognise a speaker's intended tone.

questions.explanation: A machine may translate words but miss sarcasm or affection.

questions.question: When does Tom speak to directors or writers?

options.A: When he is unsure what a character means.
options.B: When the film needs to be shortened.
options.C: When the original dialogue contains mistakes.

questions.explanation: He contacts them instead of guessing about unclear intentions.

questions.question: What does Tom advise beginners to do first?

options.B: Watch the complete film before translating it.

questions.explanation: Understanding the whole story and relationships is necessary before subtitling individual scenes.
